# Key Topics Covered

- The Titanic dataset and why it is the canonical classification example
- Data exploration before modelling (counts vs. proportions, derived variables, cross-checking predictors)
- The difference between signal strength and effect size
- Handling missing values (imputation with the mode / mean)
- Numeric vs. factor (categorical) variables — why `Pclass` must be converted
- Supervised learning and the main classification algorithms
- Train/test split and reproducible data science (`set.seed`)
- Overfitting, overtraining, data drift and model drift
- Data leakage — a subtle and dangerous modelling mistake
- Decision trees: structure, how splits are chosen, entropy and Gini, information gain
- The greedy-algorithm limitation of decision trees
- Pruning
- Confusion matrix: TP, TN, FP, FN, accuracy
- Why accuracy alone can be misleading (the cancer-model question)

# Recap From Week 2 {#sec-recap}

The class opened with a recap of Week 2, because Week 2's ideas are the foundation for Week 3.

| Week 2 idea | Why it matters this week |
|---|---|
| **Univariate vs. multivariate analysis** | Data exploration in Week 3 mixes both — single-column histograms plus two-column plots like `Fare` vs. `Pclass`. |
| **Means vs. medians on skewed data** | The Titanic `Fare` column is very skewed — the median is more meaningful than the mean. |
| **Log transformation** on the mammals dataset | Same logic applies whenever a numeric column has extreme outliers. |
| **Bimodal distributions are a red flag** | A bimodal histogram indicates two subgroups mixed in one variable (e.g. `Petal.Length` in iris — Setosa separates cleanly from the other two species). |
| **Horizontal/vertical separating line ⇒ IF-statement works** | If two classes can be separated by a horizontal or vertical line in a scatter plot, a simple condition classifies them (this is the intuition behind a **decision tree**). If separation requires a sloped line, a regression-style model is needed. |

> *Professor's note:* *"If I can draw a horizontal or vertical line, I can write an if-statement to separate the classes. If I can only draw a sloped line, I need a regression analysis."*

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Analogy — the billionaire in the room

If everyone in a room earns €30,000–€100,000, the **mean** and **median** salary are almost the same. If a billionaire walks in, the mean jumps to ~€200 million but the median barely moves. This is why the **median is better for skewed data** — it describes a *typical* person, not a mathematical average distorted by outliers.
:::

---

# The Titanic Dataset

## The question we are trying to answer

> **Can we use machine learning to predict which passengers survived the Titanic shipwreck, based only on information known about them before the ship sank?**

This is a **binary classification** problem — for every passenger, the model outputs one of two classes: `1` (survived) or `0` (did not survive).

## The variables

| Variable | Meaning | Type | Missing? | Professor's first-pass verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `PassengerId` | Unique row ID | int | 0 | **Drop** — unique per row, has no predictive meaning |
| `Survived` | 0 = no, 1 = yes | int → factor | 0 | **Target variable** |
| `Pclass` | Ticket class (1, 2, 3) | int → factor | 0 | **Keep** — very important, *must convert to factor* |
| `Name` | Passenger name | chr | 0 | **Drop** — unique per row |
| `Sex` | Gender | chr | 0 | **Keep** — strongest predictor |
| `Age` | Age in years | num | **177** | **Keep**, but needs imputation |
| `SibSp` | # siblings / spouses aboard | int | 0 | Combine into `family` |
| `Parch` | # parents / children aboard | int | 0 | Combine into `family` |
| `Ticket` | Ticket number | chr | 0 | **Drop** — too many unique values |
| `Fare` | Passenger fare | num | 0 | Keep, but may duplicate `Pclass` |
| `Cabin` | Cabin number | chr | **687** | **Drop** — too many missing |
| `Embarked` | Port of embarkation (C/Q/S) | chr | 2 | Needs fixing, then keep |

## Loading the data

```r
library(tidyverse)
library(psych)

if (!require("titanic"))
  devtools::install_github("paulhendricks/titanic")
library("titanic")

data(titanic_train)
str(titanic_train)     # structure of the data frame
```

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Exam tip — always profile a dataset before modelling

Run these two checks **on every dataset**, before you plot or model anything:

```r
# 1. Count unique values per column
uniq_len <- function(x) { length(unique(x)) }
sapply(titanic_train, uniq_len)

# 2. Count blanks (NA or empty string) per column
countBlank <- function(x) { sum(is.na(x) | x == "") }
sapply(titanic_train, countBlank)
```

Look for:

- Columns where `unique count == nrow` → IDs, drop them
- Columns with a high blank count → impute or drop
- Columns where the unique count is **one more than expected** (e.g. `Embarked` shows 4 when it should be 3) → hidden blank entries
:::

---

# Data Exploration — Survival Patterns

The professor worked through each variable methodically. Every finding below came from a plot, and every plot had **both a count view and a proportion view** side by side.

## Why plot counts AND proportions?

This is one of the most emphasised ideas of the class. It appears again in Week 4 and is likely exam material.

| | What it tells you | Without it, you risk… |
|---|---|---|
| **Count plot** | How many records fall into each group — the **effect size** | Being fooled by strong signals in tiny groups |
| **Proportion plot** | How different the group's survival rate is from the overall 38% baseline — the **signal strength** | Missing a real pattern because the group is small |

> *Professor's note:* *"If I had a ticket class four where the signal was very strong — 90/10 — but it only applied to 10 or 12 records, the signal is strong but the effect is small because it only applies to a small number of records."*

::: {.callout-warning}
## ⚠️ Common mistake

Reporting a proportion without reporting the count. A 90% survival rate in a group of 8 passengers is statistically meaningless.
:::

## Family size vs. survival

A derived variable `family = SibSp + Parch` was created:

```r
titanic_train$family   <- titanic_train$SibSp + titanic_train$Parch
titanic_train$Survived <- factor(titanic_train$Survived)

ggplot(titanic_train, aes(x = family, fill = Survived)) +
  geom_bar(stat = 'count', position = 'dodge') +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = min(titanic_train$family):max(titanic_train$family)) +
  labs(x = 'Family Size', title = 'Family size vs Survived')
```

**Finding:** Solo travellers had the worst survival rate. Families of 1–3 extra members had the best. Very large families (6+) had too few records to draw conclusions from.

**Interpretation:** Family travel correlates with children being present, and children were prioritised for lifeboats.

## Ticket class vs. survival

| Class | Approximate survival rate |
|---|---|
| 1st (Pclass = 1) | ~63% |
| 2nd (Pclass = 2) | ~47% |
| 3rd (Pclass = 3) | ~24% |

**Baseline:** 38% of all passengers survived.

## Gender vs. survival

| Sex | Survival rate |
|---|---|
| Female | ~74% |
| Male | ~19% |

> *Professor's note:* *"Gender is even more strongly predictive than ticket class. I fully expect that whatever machine learning model I use, gender will be its most important feature."*

## Fixing `Embarked`

Two passengers have blank `Embarked`. Because only two records are involved and Southampton is by far the dominant port, the blanks are assigned `S`:

```r
table(titanic_train$Embarked)
#   C   Q   S
# 2 168  77 644       # the "2" is the blank category

titanic_train[titanic_train$Embarked == "", ]$Embarked <- 'S'
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## ⚠️ Why not create a new "Unknown" category?

With only 2 records, the algorithm might **memorise** them — if both survived, the model would learn "blank `Embarked` ⇒ survive." This is a **hallucinated pattern** — 2 records cannot support a real signal. The safe choice is to merge them into the majority class (the **mode**).

If there were 20+ missing records, treating them as their own category (`"Unknown"`) would be more defensible.
:::

## Fare vs. survival

A box plot of `Fare` by `Survived` shows that survivors paid somewhat higher fares — but this is almost entirely a proxy for `Pclass`. Higher class = higher fare.

## Age vs. survival — why the raw variable hides the signal

A plain box plot of `Age` vs. `Survived` shows **almost no difference** in the median age of survivors vs. non-survivors. The signal is **not** "older = less likely to survive" — it is "under ~13 = more likely to survive."

**Fix:** create a derived binary variable `is_child`:

```r
temp <- titanic_train[!is.na(titanic_train$Age), ]
temp$is_child <- ifelse(temp$Age < 17, 1, 0)
```

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Key concept — derived variables

If a numeric variable has a **threshold effect** (the relationship changes sharply at some point), the raw value may hide the signal. Transform it:

- Age → `is_child` (binary)
- Income → quartiles or log-transformed income
- Date → day-of-week, month-of-year

This helps the algorithm "see" a pattern that would otherwise be diluted across a continuous range.
:::

## Investigating relationships *between* predictors

The Cherbourg passengers had an unusually high survival rate. Professor's theory: maybe more first-class passengers boarded there. To test this, plot `Embarked` against `Pclass` (target variable ignored):

```r
titanic_train$Pclass <- as.factor(titanic_train$Pclass)

ggplot(titanic_train, aes(Embarked, fill = Pclass)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  ggtitle("Ticket class by Embarkation")
```

**Result (confirmed):** About 50% of Cherbourg passengers were first class. Almost 100% of Queenstown passengers were third class.

> *Professor's note:* *"Whether you survived the Titanic or not should have nothing to do with where you got on the boat. `Embarked` is a proxy for `Pclass`. I would strongly consider removing `Embarked` from my model entirely."*

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Exam tip — proxy variables

A variable that appears predictive may actually be a **proxy** for another variable that is already in the model. Cross-check pairs of predictors, not just predictor-vs-target relationships. This is an example of **collinearity**.
:::

## Pairs plot — correlation between predictors

```r
columns <- c("Pclass", "Age", "Fare", "family")
pairs.panels(titanic_train[, columns], method = "pearson",
             hist.col = "#00AFBB", density = TRUE, ellipses = FALSE)
```

Strongest correlation: `Pclass` vs. `Fare` = **−0.55** (higher class = lower number = higher fare).

---

# Numeric vs. Factor — A Subtle But Critical Point

`Pclass` stores the values `1`, `2`, `3`. To a human they are categories. To a machine learning algorithm they look numeric.

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Analogy — money vs. ticket class

If you pay **€2** and I pay **€4**, it is true that I paid 100% more than you.
If you are in **ticket class 2** and I am in **ticket class 1**, it is **not** true that I am "100% more first-class than you." These are **categories with ordering**, not mathematical amounts.

Treating categories as numbers lets algorithms do nonsense arithmetic on them.
:::

**Rule of thumb:** If a column uses numbers to encode categories, convert it:

```r
titanic_train$Pclass   <- as.factor(titanic_train$Pclass)
titanic_train$Survived <- as.factor(titanic_train$Survived)
```

---

# Supervised Learning & Classification

## Definitions

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Supervised learning** | Training a model on labelled historical data so it can predict labels for new data. |
| **Classification** | Predicting a **category** (e.g. survived / not survived). |
| **Binary classification** | Two possible outcomes. |
| **Multi-class classification** | 3+ outcomes (e.g. iris species). |
| **Regression** | Predicting a **continuous number** (next week's topic). |

## Popular classification algorithms

| Algorithm | Strength |
|---|---|
| Decision Tree | Interpretable, good for exploration |
| Logistic Regression | Probabilistic, simple baseline (Week 4) |
| Naive Bayes | Fast, works well with text |
| Support Vector Machines (SVM) | Strong in high dimensions |
| Random Forest | Ensemble of trees, handles interactions |
| Gradient Boosted Trees (GBM) | Often best on tabular data |
| Deep Learning | Best for images, text, audio |

## The classification workflow

1. Collect a **historical dataset** where the outcome is already known.
2. Apply a **machine learning algorithm**.
3. Produce a **model**.
4. In the future, feed new data (predictors only) to the model and get a **prediction**.

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Data leakage — critical exam concept

**All predictor variables must be available at the moment the prediction is made.** A field that only becomes known *after* the outcome has happened cannot be a predictor — that is **data leakage**.

**Example from the lecture:** using "credit card cancelled?" to predict "is this transaction fraudulent?" The cancellation typically happens *because* of the fraud, so including it leaks the answer.

Data leakage produces models that look amazing on paper and fail disastrously in production.
:::

---

# Building a Model — The Minimum Template

This is the pattern **every** classification task in this module will follow. The only lines that change between algorithms are the ones marked ⚠️.

```r
# (1) Load package and data
library(rpart)
data(iris)

# (2) Reproducibility — set the random seed
set.seed(1987)

# (3) Train/test split (90/10 here)
nobs  <- nrow(iris)
train <- sample(nobs, 0.9 * nobs)
test  <- setdiff(seq_len(nobs), train)

# (4) ⚠️ Fit the model (this line changes per algorithm)
rpart <- rpart(Species ~ ., data = iris[train, ], method = "class")

# (5) ⚠️ Predict on the TEST set (this line may change per algorithm)
pr <- predict(rpart, newdata = iris[test, ], type = "class")

# (6) Confusion matrix + accuracy
tab <- table(pr, iris[test, ]$Species, dnn = c("Predicted", "Actual"))
print(tab)

accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab)) / sum(tab)
sprintf("Model accuracy on test data = %.1f%%", accuracy)
```

> *Professor's note:* *"The only two lines that will change for GBM, random forest, or logistic regression are the two I've marked. Everything else is boilerplate."*

## Why `set.seed`?

`set.seed(1987)` fixes the random number generator. Without it, `sample()` returns different rows every time the script runs, so the train/test split changes and the accuracy changes.

With it, **anyone running the script on any machine gets identical results** — this is the principle of **reproducible data science**.

> *Professor's note:* *"If scientific papers report statistically significant results but other people cannot reproduce their analysis, then there is a question mark over the paper itself."*

## Train / test split — the absolute rules

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ The non-negotiable rules of a train/test split

1. **No row appears in both sets.**
2. **Every row appears in exactly one set.**
3. The model is **fitted only on the train set**.
4. Evaluation metrics are **computed only on the test set**.
5. Any transformation (imputation, scaling) is **calculated from the train set** and then applied to both sets.
:::

## Optional: stratified sampling

When classes are imbalanced, a random split may put too few of the minority class into the test set. **Stratified sampling** fixes this by splitting within each class.

In iris (50/50/50) and Titanic (~38% survived) it is not critical. In something like image classification with 1,000 classes where some classes have only 10 images, it is essential.

## Overfitting vs. generalisation

| Term | What it means |
|---|---|
| **Generalisation** | The model performs well on data it has never seen. |
| **Overfitting / overtraining** | The model has *memorised* the training data — including its noise — instead of learning the true pattern. |
| **Symptom** | Very high accuracy on train, much lower on test. |

## Model drift and data drift

| Term | Meaning | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Data drift** | The distribution of the input data changes over time (e.g. new customers look different from old ones). | Retrain periodically on fresh data. |
| **Model drift** | The *relationship* between inputs and the target changes over time. | Retrain periodically on fresh data. |

> *Professor's example:* *"Facial recognition systems trained on under-representative datasets performed poorly in production for women and minorities. That is data drift — the data distribution in production was significantly different from the training distribution."*

---

# Decision Trees

## What they are

A **decision tree** is a flowchart of yes/no questions. Each internal node asks a question about one variable, and the record moves to the left branch if the answer is true and to the right branch if false. The **leaf nodes** give the final predicted class.

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Analogy — 20 Questions

A decision tree is like the game *20 Questions*. Each question ("Is it an animal?", "Does it fly?") narrows down the possibilities. A good player asks the most informative question first — exactly what a decision tree algorithm does.
:::

## How the tree is built

```text
Start with all training data at the ROOT.
Repeat:
    1. Find the best column and best split condition.
    2. Split the data into two branches.
    3. For each branch: if all samples have the same class, make it a LEAF.
       Otherwise, repeat step 1 on that branch.
Stop when:
    - All leaves are pure, OR
    - No useful column remains to split on, OR
    - A heuristic stopping rule fires (e.g. too few rows, too little gain).
```

## Choosing the best split — entropy, Gini, information gain

At each node, the algorithm asks: *"Which column-and-condition produces the purest child nodes?"* It scores every candidate split using an **impurity measure**.

**Entropy** — measures disorder (0 = pure, 1 = perfectly mixed in a binary problem):

$$\text{Entropy}(t) \;=\; -\sum_{i=0}^{c-1} p(i \mid t) \log_2 p(i \mid t)$$

**Gini index** — same idea, slightly different formula:

$$\text{Gini}(t) \;=\; 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{c-1} \big[ p(i \mid t) \big]^2$$

Where `c` = number of classes and `p(i|t)` = probability that a record at node `t` belongs to class `i`.

**Information gain** = impurity of the parent − weighted average impurity of the children. The split with the **highest information gain** is chosen.

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Intuition — entropy at a glance

| Node composition | Entropy | Purity |
|---|---|---|
| 100% class A, 0% class B | 0 | Perfect |
| 80% class A, 20% class B | 0.72 | Fairly pure |
| 50% class A, 50% class B | 1.0 | Maximum impurity |
:::

## Handling numeric variables

Decision trees only understand yes/no questions, so a numeric variable like `Age` is split on a **threshold** the algorithm chooses automatically, e.g. `Age >= 13`.

## Pruning

A deep tree will eventually memorise the training set — classic overfitting. **Pruning** cuts off some lower branches to force the tree to generalise.

## Advantages and disadvantages

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Highly interpretable — can be drawn and explained | Usually beaten by random forests / GBMs in accuracy |
| Can be written as IF-THEN rules for any language | Prone to overfitting — needs pruning |
| Handles both categorical and numeric data | Handles missing data poorly |
| No need to scale or normalise variables | **Greedy** — poor at capturing variable *interactions* |
| Great as an exploratory tool | Sensitive to small changes in the training data |

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ The "greedy algorithm" limitation

A decision tree picks the **single best column at each node**. Once it splits on `Sex = male`, the algorithm can never again consider combinations like "female AND 3rd class AND paid high fare" as one decision — each branch is handled in isolation.

This is why **Random Forest** (many trees, some trained on column subsets) and **GBM** (sequential trees correcting each other) outperform single decision trees on most real problems.
:::

## Why use decision trees in real life?

> *Professor's note:* *"I always consider decision trees and logistic regression at the start of a project — not as the final model, but as a form of data exploration. If the tree shows me something surprising or a red flag, I go back and fix my data."*

Decision trees are chosen over more accurate models when **explainability is legally or ethically required**:

- Credit scoring / loan approval (you must be able to tell a rejected applicant *why*)
- Medical decision support
- Anywhere a regulator or court could demand a justification

---

# The Lab — Titanic Decision Tree

## Setup

```r
library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)
library(GGally)
library(titanic)

data(titanic_train)
set.seed(42)                     # note: lab uses 42, not 1987

nobs  <- nrow(titanic_train)
train <- sample(nobs, 0.9 * nobs)    # 801 rows
test  <- setdiff(seq_len(nobs), train) # 90 rows

target <- "Survived"
```

## What happens if you leave `Ticket` in?

The professor **deliberately leaves `Ticket` in** to show how decision trees fail spectacularly when given high-cardinality columns.

```r
colsToIgnore <- c("Name")     # intentionally does NOT drop Ticket, Cabin, PassengerId
input <- setdiff(names(titanic_train), c(target, colsToIgnore))

rpart_bad <- rpart(Survived ~ .,
                   data = titanic_train[train, c(input, target)],
                   method = "class")
rpart.plot(rpart_bad)    # tree is a mess of ticket-number splits

# Predicting fails with:
#   Error: factor Ticket has new levels 110564, 111369, 111427, ...
pr <- predict(rpart_bad, newdata = titanic_train[test, c(input, target)], type = "class")
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## ⚠️ Common mistake — two failure modes at once

1. **Memorisation**: the tree splits on individual ticket numbers — pure overfitting.
2. **Unseen-category crash**: when the test set contains a ticket number the model has never seen, `predict()` throws an error: *"factor Ticket has new levels …"*

This is why high-cardinality ID-like columns (`Ticket`, `Cabin`, `Name`, `PassengerId`) **must be dropped before fitting**.
:::

## The correct setup

```r
colsToIgnore <- c("PassengerId", "Name", "Ticket", "Cabin")
input <- setdiff(names(titanic_train), c(target, colsToIgnore))

rpart_model <- rpart(Survived ~ .,
                     data = titanic_train[train, c(input, target)],
                     method = "class")
rpart.plot(rpart_model, main = "Decision Tree titanic_train $ Survived")
```

## Reading the tree

The top splits match exactly what the exploratory analysis predicted:

1. **Sex = male?** — the most informative first question. Most males did not survive.
2. Down the *male* branch: **Age ≥ 13?** Younger boys had better odds. Further split on **SibSp ≥ 3**.
3. Down the *female* branch: **Pclass ≥ 3?** Third-class women had a mixed outcome; 1st- and 2nd-class women almost all survived.
4. Further splits on **Fare** inside the 3rd-class-female branch.

> *Professor's note:* *"I don't see any red flags. There's nothing in this graph that is a surprise given the data exploration I already did. That is exactly what I want."*

## Predicting and evaluating

```r
pr <- predict(rpart_model,
              newdata = titanic_train[test, c(input, target)],
              type = "class")

tab <- table(pr, titanic_train[test, ]$Survived,
             dnn = c("Predicted", "Actual"))
print(tab)
#            Actual
# Predicted   0   1
#         0  50  19
#         1   4  17

accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab)) / sum(tab)
sprintf("Accuracy = %.1f%%", accuracy)
# "Accuracy = 74.4%"
```

---

# The Confusion Matrix

For binary classification, the four possible outcomes are:

|  | **Actual = 0** (did NOT survive) | **Actual = 1** (DID survive) |
|---|---|---|
| **Predicted = 0** | True Negative (**TN**) | False Negative (**FN**) |
| **Predicted = 1** | False Positive (**FP**) | True Positive (**TP**) |

For the Titanic model above:

| Cell | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| TN | 50 | Correctly said "did not survive" |
| FN | 19 | Said "did not survive" but they actually did |
| FP | 4 | Said "survived" but they actually didn't |
| TP | 17 | Correctly said "survived" |

## The accuracy formula

$$\text{Accuracy} \;=\; \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} \;=\; \frac{17 + 50}{17 + 50 + 4 + 19} \;=\; \frac{67}{90} \;\approx\; 74\%$$

## Computing TN/FP/FN/TP in R

```r
testData <- titanic_train[test, ]
testData$pr <- pr

TN <- sum(testData$Survived == 0 & testData$pr == 0)
FP <- sum(testData$Survived == 0 & testData$pr == 1)
FN <- sum(testData$Survived == 1 & testData$pr == 0)
TP <- sum(testData$Survived == 1 & testData$pr == 1)
```

## Class-wise performance

Look at the confusion matrix by row and column:

| | Did NOT survive (Actual = 0) | DID survive (Actual = 1) |
|---|---|---|
| Total in test set | 54 | 36 |
| Correctly classified | 50 (93%) | 17 (47%) |

**The model is much better at spotting non-survivors than survivors.** Overall accuracy of 74% hides this imbalance.

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Exam-style thought question — carry this into Week 4 {#sec-thought-question}

> *Professor's question (paraphrased):* *"Suppose someone builds a model to predict whether a patient has cancer, and reports 90% overall accuracy. Is this a good model? Would you put it in production?"*

**Hint — think about the base rate.** If only 1 in 100 people in the screening population actually has cancer, a model that **always says "no cancer"** will be 99% accurate and **miss every single sick person**. Accuracy alone is dangerous when classes are imbalanced.

Week 4 introduces **precision, recall, and F1** — the metrics that expose this problem.
:::

---

# Handling Missing Values

`Age` has 177 missing values (~20% of the column). Three reasonable options:

| Option | When to use it | Downside |
|---|---|---|
| **Drop the column** | The column is not important and has lots of missing values | Lose a potentially useful predictor |
| **Drop the rows with missing values** | Very few rows affected | Loses data; fails for new rows with missing values at prediction time |
| **Impute (fill in) the missing values** | Default choice for important columns | Imputed values carry no real information, just keep the row usable |

For Titanic's `Age`, imputation is correct.

## Mean imputation — done correctly

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Exam-critical detail

The mean **must be calculated on the training set only**, then applied to both train and test sets.

Calculating the mean on the full dataset (train + test combined) is a form of **data leakage** — information from the test set has bled into the training process.
:::

```r
# Correct: mean is computed from TRAIN only (na.rm = TRUE ignores NAs)
meanAge <- mean(titanic_train[train, ]$Age, na.rm = TRUE)
meanAge
# 29.725 (approximately)

# Apply to the WHOLE dataset — so both train and test rows have Age filled in
titanic_train[is.na(titanic_train$Age), ]$Age <- meanAge
```

After imputation, the Titanic decision tree's accuracy improves from **74.4% to 75.5%** — a small but real gain, and far more important for algorithms like logistic regression that refuse to run with NAs.

## Mode imputation for categorical columns

For the 2 blank `Embarked` values, the same principle applies — use the **mode** (most common category) of the training set:

```r
titanic_train[titanic_train$Embarked == "", ]$Embarked <- 'S'
```

---

# Scenario → Concept Reference Table

Exam-style situations and what to do about them:

| Scenario | Correct action | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| A column has a unique value for every row (e.g. `PassengerId`, `Name`, `Ticket`) | Drop it | No generalisable pattern — tree will memorise |
| A column has 5–10% missing values and is important | Impute (mean for numeric, mode for categorical) on **train only** | Preserves rows; avoids data leakage |
| A column has ~70% missing values (e.g. `Cabin`) | Drop it | Too little real information left |
| Categorical variable encoded as numbers (`Pclass` = 1, 2, 3) | Convert with `as.factor()` | Stop the algorithm doing arithmetic on categories |
| Numeric variable with a threshold effect (e.g. age and children) | Create a derived binary variable | Makes the threshold visible to the algorithm |
| Two predictors are highly correlated (e.g. `Pclass` and `Fare`) | Consider dropping one | Redundant information; algorithm will pick one and ignore the other |
| `Embarked` shows a strong signal but seems implausible | Cross-check against other predictors | Usually it is a proxy for the real cause |
| Test accuracy ≫ training accuracy on a tree | Suspicious — could indicate a buggy split | Rare; investigate before trusting |
| Training accuracy ≫ test accuracy | **Overfitting** — prune the tree or use a simpler model | Classic symptom |
| New categorical values appear at prediction time that weren't in training | Pipeline error — usually means the variable shouldn't have been used | Decision tree cannot handle unseen categories |
| Predictor includes a field only known after the target event (e.g. "card cancelled" when predicting fraud) | Remove it immediately | **Data leakage** — model will be useless in production |

---

# Summary Points — Week 3 Takeaways

- ✅ **Always profile a dataset before modelling**: count unique values and blanks in every column.
- ✅ **Plot counts AND proportions** when comparing a predictor to the target.
- ✅ **Counts show effect size; proportions show signal strength.** You need both.
- ✅ **Create derived variables** (`is_child`, `family`) when raw variables have threshold effects.
- ✅ **Cross-check predictors against each other**, not just against the target. Watch for proxy variables.
- ✅ **Convert ordinal/categorical numeric codes to factors** with `as.factor()`.
- ✅ **Always set a random seed** for reproducible data science.
- ✅ **Split into train and test before anything else.** No row in both. No exceptions.
- ✅ **Fit the model on train only. Evaluate on test only.**
- ✅ **Compute imputation values from the train set only**, then apply to both sets.
- ✅ **Watch for data leakage** — any predictor that would not be available at prediction time must be removed.
- ✅ **Drop ID-like columns** (`PassengerId`, `Ticket`, `Name`, `Cabin`) before fitting a decision tree.
- ✅ **Decision trees are greedy** — they are great for interpretation and poor at capturing variable interactions.
- ✅ **Overall accuracy can mislead.** Always look at the full confusion matrix, especially with imbalanced classes.

# Recap From Week 2 {#sec-recap}

The class opened with a recap of Week 2, because Week 2's ideas are the foundation for Week 3.

| Week 2 idea | Why it matters this week |
|---|---|
| **Univariate vs. multivariate analysis** | Data exploration in Week 3 mixes both — single-column histograms plus two-column plots like `Fare` vs. `Pclass`. |
| **Means vs. medians on skewed data** | The Titanic `Fare` column is very skewed — the median is more meaningful than the mean. |
| **Log transformation** on the mammals dataset | Same logic applies whenever a numeric column has extreme outliers. |
| **Bimodal distributions are a red flag** | A bimodal histogram indicates two subgroups mixed in one variable (e.g. `Petal.Length` in iris — Setosa separates cleanly from the other two species). |
| **Horizontal/vertical separating line ⇒ IF-statement works** | If two classes can be separated by a horizontal or vertical line in a scatter plot, a simple condition classifies them (this is the intuition behind a **decision tree**). If separation requires a sloped line, a regression-style model is needed. |

> *Professor's note:* *"If I can draw a horizontal or vertical line, I can write an if-statement to separate the classes. If I can only draw a sloped line, I need a regression analysis."*

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Analogy — the billionaire in the room

If everyone in a room earns €30,000–€100,000, the **mean** and **median** salary are almost the same. If a billionaire walks in, the mean jumps to ~€200 million but the median barely moves. This is why the **median is better for skewed data** — it describes a *typical* person, not a mathematical average distorted by outliers.
:::

---

# The Titanic Dataset

## The question we are trying to answer

> **Can we use machine learning to predict which passengers survived the Titanic shipwreck, based only on information known about them before the ship sank?**

This is a **binary classification** problem — for every passenger, the model outputs one of two classes: `1` (survived) or `0` (did not survive).

## The variables

| Variable | Meaning | Type | Missing? | Professor's first-pass verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `PassengerId` | Unique row ID | int | 0 | **Drop** — unique per row, has no predictive meaning |
| `Survived` | 0 = no, 1 = yes | int → factor | 0 | **Target variable** |
| `Pclass` | Ticket class (1, 2, 3) | int → factor | 0 | **Keep** — very important, *must convert to factor* |
| `Name` | Passenger name | chr | 0 | **Drop** — unique per row |
| `Sex` | Gender | chr | 0 | **Keep** — strongest predictor |
| `Age` | Age in years | num | **177** | **Keep**, but needs imputation |
| `SibSp` | # siblings / spouses aboard | int | 0 | Combine into `family` |
| `Parch` | # parents / children aboard | int | 0 | Combine into `family` |
| `Ticket` | Ticket number | chr | 0 | **Drop** — too many unique values |
| `Fare` | Passenger fare | num | 0 | Keep, but may duplicate `Pclass` |
| `Cabin` | Cabin number | chr | **687** | **Drop** — too many missing |
| `Embarked` | Port of embarkation (C/Q/S) | chr | 2 | Needs fixing, then keep |

## Loading the data

```r
library(tidyverse)
library(psych)

if (!require("titanic"))
  devtools::install_github("paulhendricks/titanic")
library("titanic")

data(titanic_train)
str(titanic_train)     # structure of the data frame
```

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Exam tip — always profile a dataset before modelling

Run these two checks **on every dataset**, before you plot or model anything:

```r
# 1. Count unique values per column
uniq_len <- function(x) { length(unique(x)) }
sapply(titanic_train, uniq_len)

# 2. Count blanks (NA or empty string) per column
countBlank <- function(x) { sum(is.na(x) | x == "") }
sapply(titanic_train, countBlank)
```

Look for:

- Columns where `unique count == nrow` → IDs, drop them
- Columns with a high blank count → impute or drop
- Columns where the unique count is **one more than expected** (e.g. `Embarked` shows 4 when it should be 3) → hidden blank entries
:::

---

# Data Exploration — Survival Patterns

The professor worked through each variable methodically. Every finding below came from a plot, and every plot had **both a count view and a proportion view** side by side.

## Why plot counts AND proportions?

This is one of the most emphasised ideas of the class. It appears again in Week 4 and is likely exam material.

| | What it tells you | Without it, you risk… |
|---|---|---|
| **Count plot** | How many records fall into each group — the **effect size** | Being fooled by strong signals in tiny groups |
| **Proportion plot** | How different the group's survival rate is from the overall 38% baseline — the **signal strength** | Missing a real pattern because the group is small |

> *Professor's note:* *"If I had a ticket class four where the signal was very strong — 90/10 — but it only applied to 10 or 12 records, the signal is strong but the effect is small because it only applies to a small number of records."*

::: {.callout-warning}
## ⚠️ Common mistake

Reporting a proportion without reporting the count. A 90% survival rate in a group of 8 passengers is statistically meaningless.
:::

## Family size vs. survival

A derived variable `family = SibSp + Parch` was created:

```r
titanic_train$family   <- titanic_train$SibSp + titanic_train$Parch
titanic_train$Survived <- factor(titanic_train$Survived)

ggplot(titanic_train, aes(x = family, fill = Survived)) +
  geom_bar(stat = 'count', position = 'dodge') +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = min(titanic_train$family):max(titanic_train$family)) +
  labs(x = 'Family Size', title = 'Family size vs Survived')
```

**Finding:** Solo travellers had the worst survival rate. Families of 1–3 extra members had the best. Very large families (6+) had too few records to draw conclusions from.

**Interpretation:** Family travel correlates with children being present, and children were prioritised for lifeboats.

## Ticket class vs. survival

| Class | Approximate survival rate |
|---|---|
| 1st (Pclass = 1) | ~63% |
| 2nd (Pclass = 2) | ~47% |
| 3rd (Pclass = 3) | ~24% |

**Baseline:** 38% of all passengers survived.

## Gender vs. survival

| Sex | Survival rate |
|---|---|
| Female | ~74% |
| Male | ~19% |

> *Professor's note:* *"Gender is even more strongly predictive than ticket class. I fully expect that whatever machine learning model I use, gender will be its most important feature."*

## Fixing `Embarked`

Two passengers have blank `Embarked`. Because only two records are involved and Southampton is by far the dominant port, the blanks are assigned `S`:

```r
table(titanic_train$Embarked)
#   C   Q   S
# 2 168  77 644       # the "2" is the blank category

titanic_train[titanic_train$Embarked == "", ]$Embarked <- 'S'
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## ⚠️ Why not create a new "Unknown" category?

With only 2 records, the algorithm might **memorise** them — if both survived, the model would learn "blank `Embarked` ⇒ survive." This is a **hallucinated pattern** — 2 records cannot support a real signal. The safe choice is to merge them into the majority class (the **mode**).

If there were 20+ missing records, treating them as their own category (`"Unknown"`) would be more defensible.
:::

## Fare vs. survival

A box plot of `Fare` by `Survived` shows that survivors paid somewhat higher fares — but this is almost entirely a proxy for `Pclass`. Higher class = higher fare.

## Age vs. survival — why the raw variable hides the signal

A plain box plot of `Age` vs. `Survived` shows **almost no difference** in the median age of survivors vs. non-survivors. The signal is **not** "older = less likely to survive" — it is "under ~13 = more likely to survive."

**Fix:** create a derived binary variable `is_child`:

```r
temp <- titanic_train[!is.na(titanic_train$Age), ]
temp$is_child <- ifelse(temp$Age < 17, 1, 0)
```

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Key concept — derived variables

If a numeric variable has a **threshold effect** (the relationship changes sharply at some point), the raw value may hide the signal. Transform it:

- Age → `is_child` (binary)
- Income → quartiles or log-transformed income
- Date → day-of-week, month-of-year

This helps the algorithm "see" a pattern that would otherwise be diluted across a continuous range.
:::

## Investigating relationships *between* predictors

The Cherbourg passengers had an unusually high survival rate. Professor's theory: maybe more first-class passengers boarded there. To test this, plot `Embarked` against `Pclass` (target variable ignored):

```r
titanic_train$Pclass <- as.factor(titanic_train$Pclass)

ggplot(titanic_train, aes(Embarked, fill = Pclass)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  ggtitle("Ticket class by Embarkation")
```

**Result (confirmed):** About 50% of Cherbourg passengers were first class. Almost 100% of Queenstown passengers were third class.

> *Professor's note:* *"Whether you survived the Titanic or not should have nothing to do with where you got on the boat. `Embarked` is a proxy for `Pclass`. I would strongly consider removing `Embarked` from my model entirely."*

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Exam tip — proxy variables

A variable that appears predictive may actually be a **proxy** for another variable that is already in the model. Cross-check pairs of predictors, not just predictor-vs-target relationships. This is an example of **collinearity**.
:::

## Pairs plot — correlation between predictors

```r
columns <- c("Pclass", "Age", "Fare", "family")
pairs.panels(titanic_train[, columns], method = "pearson",
             hist.col = "#00AFBB", density = TRUE, ellipses = FALSE)
```

Strongest correlation: `Pclass` vs. `Fare` = **−0.55** (higher class = lower number = higher fare).

---

# Numeric vs. Factor — A Subtle But Critical Point

`Pclass` stores the values `1`, `2`, `3`. To a human they are categories. To a machine learning algorithm they look numeric.

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Analogy — money vs. ticket class

If you pay **€2** and I pay **€4**, it is true that I paid 100% more than you.
If you are in **ticket class 2** and I am in **ticket class 1**, it is **not** true that I am "100% more first-class than you." These are **categories with ordering**, not mathematical amounts.

Treating categories as numbers lets algorithms do nonsense arithmetic on them.
:::

**Rule of thumb:** If a column uses numbers to encode categories, convert it:

```r
titanic_train$Pclass   <- as.factor(titanic_train$Pclass)
titanic_train$Survived <- as.factor(titanic_train$Survived)
```

---

# Supervised Learning & Classification

## Definitions

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Supervised learning** | Training a model on labelled historical data so it can predict labels for new data. |
| **Classification** | Predicting a **category** (e.g. survived / not survived). |
| **Binary classification** | Two possible outcomes. |
| **Multi-class classification** | 3+ outcomes (e.g. iris species). |
| **Regression** | Predicting a **continuous number** (next week's topic). |

## Popular classification algorithms

| Algorithm | Strength |
|---|---|
| Decision Tree | Interpretable, good for exploration |
| Logistic Regression | Probabilistic, simple baseline (Week 4) |
| Naive Bayes | Fast, works well with text |
| Support Vector Machines (SVM) | Strong in high dimensions |
| Random Forest | Ensemble of trees, handles interactions |
| Gradient Boosted Trees (GBM) | Often best on tabular data |
| Deep Learning | Best for images, text, audio |

## The classification workflow

1. Collect a **historical dataset** where the outcome is already known.
2. Apply a **machine learning algorithm**.
3. Produce a **model**.
4. In the future, feed new data (predictors only) to the model and get a **prediction**.

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Data leakage — critical exam concept

**All predictor variables must be available at the moment the prediction is made.** A field that only becomes known *after* the outcome has happened cannot be a predictor — that is **data leakage**.

**Example from the lecture:** using "credit card cancelled?" to predict "is this transaction fraudulent?" The cancellation typically happens *because* of the fraud, so including it leaks the answer.

Data leakage produces models that look amazing on paper and fail disastrously in production.
:::

---

# Building a Model — The Minimum Template

This is the pattern **every** classification task in this module will follow. The only lines that change between algorithms are the ones marked ⚠️.

```r
# (1) Load package and data
library(rpart)
data(iris)

# (2) Reproducibility — set the random seed
set.seed(1987)

# (3) Train/test split (90/10 here)
nobs  <- nrow(iris)
train <- sample(nobs, 0.9 * nobs)
test  <- setdiff(seq_len(nobs), train)

# (4) ⚠️ Fit the model (this line changes per algorithm)
rpart <- rpart(Species ~ ., data = iris[train, ], method = "class")

# (5) ⚠️ Predict on the TEST set (this line may change per algorithm)
pr <- predict(rpart, newdata = iris[test, ], type = "class")

# (6) Confusion matrix + accuracy
tab <- table(pr, iris[test, ]$Species, dnn = c("Predicted", "Actual"))
print(tab)

accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab)) / sum(tab)
sprintf("Model accuracy on test data = %.1f%%", accuracy)
```

> *Professor's note:* *"The only two lines that will change for GBM, random forest, or logistic regression are the two I've marked. Everything else is boilerplate."*

## Why `set.seed`?

`set.seed(1987)` fixes the random number generator. Without it, `sample()` returns different rows every time the script runs, so the train/test split changes and the accuracy changes.

With it, **anyone running the script on any machine gets identical results** — this is the principle of **reproducible data science**.

> *Professor's note:* *"If scientific papers report statistically significant results but other people cannot reproduce their analysis, then there is a question mark over the paper itself."*

## Train / test split — the absolute rules

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ The non-negotiable rules of a train/test split

1. **No row appears in both sets.**
2. **Every row appears in exactly one set.**
3. The model is **fitted only on the train set**.
4. Evaluation metrics are **computed only on the test set**.
5. Any transformation (imputation, scaling) is **calculated from the train set** and then applied to both sets.
:::

## Optional: stratified sampling

When classes are imbalanced, a random split may put too few of the minority class into the test set. **Stratified sampling** fixes this by splitting within each class.

In iris (50/50/50) and Titanic (~38% survived) it is not critical. In something like image classification with 1,000 classes where some classes have only 10 images, it is essential.

## Overfitting vs. generalisation

| Term | What it means |
|---|---|
| **Generalisation** | The model performs well on data it has never seen. |
| **Overfitting / overtraining** | The model has *memorised* the training data — including its noise — instead of learning the true pattern. |
| **Symptom** | Very high accuracy on train, much lower on test. |

## Model drift and data drift

| Term | Meaning | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Data drift** | The distribution of the input data changes over time (e.g. new customers look different from old ones). | Retrain periodically on fresh data. |
| **Model drift** | The *relationship* between inputs and the target changes over time. | Retrain periodically on fresh data. |

> *Professor's example:* *"Facial recognition systems trained on under-representative datasets performed poorly in production for women and minorities. That is data drift — the data distribution in production was significantly different from the training distribution."*

---

# Decision Trees

## What they are

A **decision tree** is a flowchart of yes/no questions. Each internal node asks a question about one variable, and the record moves to the left branch if the answer is true and to the right branch if false. The **leaf nodes** give the final predicted class.

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Analogy — 20 Questions

A decision tree is like the game *20 Questions*. Each question ("Is it an animal?", "Does it fly?") narrows down the possibilities. A good player asks the most informative question first — exactly what a decision tree algorithm does.
:::

## How the tree is built

```text
Start with all training data at the ROOT.
Repeat:
    1. Find the best column and best split condition.
    2. Split the data into two branches.
    3. For each branch: if all samples have the same class, make it a LEAF.
       Otherwise, repeat step 1 on that branch.
Stop when:
    - All leaves are pure, OR
    - No useful column remains to split on, OR
    - A heuristic stopping rule fires (e.g. too few rows, too little gain).
```

## Choosing the best split — entropy, Gini, information gain

At each node, the algorithm asks: *"Which column-and-condition produces the purest child nodes?"* It scores every candidate split using an **impurity measure**.

**Entropy** — measures disorder (0 = pure, 1 = perfectly mixed in a binary problem):

$$\text{Entropy}(t) \;=\; -\sum_{i=0}^{c-1} p(i \mid t) \log_2 p(i \mid t)$$

**Gini index** — same idea, slightly different formula:

$$\text{Gini}(t) \;=\; 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{c-1} \big[ p(i \mid t) \big]^2$$

Where `c` = number of classes and `p(i|t)` = probability that a record at node `t` belongs to class `i`.

**Information gain** = impurity of the parent − weighted average impurity of the children. The split with the **highest information gain** is chosen.

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Intuition — entropy at a glance

| Node composition | Entropy | Purity |
|---|---|---|
| 100% class A, 0% class B | 0 | Perfect |
| 80% class A, 20% class B | 0.72 | Fairly pure |
| 50% class A, 50% class B | 1.0 | Maximum impurity |
:::

## Handling numeric variables

Decision trees only understand yes/no questions, so a numeric variable like `Age` is split on a **threshold** the algorithm chooses automatically, e.g. `Age >= 13`.

## Pruning

A deep tree will eventually memorise the training set — classic overfitting. **Pruning** cuts off some lower branches to force the tree to generalise.

## Advantages and disadvantages

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Highly interpretable — can be drawn and explained | Usually beaten by random forests / GBMs in accuracy |
| Can be written as IF-THEN rules for any language | Prone to overfitting — needs pruning |
| Handles both categorical and numeric data | Handles missing data poorly |
| No need to scale or normalise variables | **Greedy** — poor at capturing variable *interactions* |
| Great as an exploratory tool | Sensitive to small changes in the training data |

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ The "greedy algorithm" limitation

A decision tree picks the **single best column at each node**. Once it splits on `Sex = male`, the algorithm can never again consider combinations like "female AND 3rd class AND paid high fare" as one decision — each branch is handled in isolation.

This is why **Random Forest** (many trees, some trained on column subsets) and **GBM** (sequential trees correcting each other) outperform single decision trees on most real problems.
:::

## Why use decision trees in real life?

> *Professor's note:* *"I always consider decision trees and logistic regression at the start of a project — not as the final model, but as a form of data exploration. If the tree shows me something surprising or a red flag, I go back and fix my data."*

Decision trees are chosen over more accurate models when **explainability is legally or ethically required**:

- Credit scoring / loan approval (you must be able to tell a rejected applicant *why*)
- Medical decision support
- Anywhere a regulator or court could demand a justification

---

# The Lab — Titanic Decision Tree

## Setup

```r
library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)
library(GGally)
library(titanic)

data(titanic_train)
set.seed(42)                     # note: lab uses 42, not 1987

nobs  <- nrow(titanic_train)
train <- sample(nobs, 0.9 * nobs)    # 801 rows
test  <- setdiff(seq_len(nobs), train) # 90 rows

target <- "Survived"
```

## What happens if you leave `Ticket` in?

The professor **deliberately leaves `Ticket` in** to show how decision trees fail spectacularly when given high-cardinality columns.

```r
colsToIgnore <- c("Name")     # intentionally does NOT drop Ticket, Cabin, PassengerId
input <- setdiff(names(titanic_train), c(target, colsToIgnore))

rpart_bad <- rpart(Survived ~ .,
                   data = titanic_train[train, c(input, target)],
                   method = "class")
rpart.plot(rpart_bad)    # tree is a mess of ticket-number splits

# Predicting fails with:
#   Error: factor Ticket has new levels 110564, 111369, 111427, ...
pr <- predict(rpart_bad, newdata = titanic_train[test, c(input, target)], type = "class")
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## ⚠️ Common mistake — two failure modes at once

1. **Memorisation**: the tree splits on individual ticket numbers — pure overfitting.
2. **Unseen-category crash**: when the test set contains a ticket number the model has never seen, `predict()` throws an error: *"factor Ticket has new levels …"*

This is why high-cardinality ID-like columns (`Ticket`, `Cabin`, `Name`, `PassengerId`) **must be dropped before fitting**.
:::

## The correct setup

```r
colsToIgnore <- c("PassengerId", "Name", "Ticket", "Cabin")
input <- setdiff(names(titanic_train), c(target, colsToIgnore))

rpart_model <- rpart(Survived ~ .,
                     data = titanic_train[train, c(input, target)],
                     method = "class")
rpart.plot(rpart_model, main = "Decision Tree titanic_train $ Survived")
```

## Reading the tree

The top splits match exactly what the exploratory analysis predicted:

1. **Sex = male?** — the most informative first question. Most males did not survive.
2. Down the *male* branch: **Age ≥ 13?** Younger boys had better odds. Further split on **SibSp ≥ 3**.
3. Down the *female* branch: **Pclass ≥ 3?** Third-class women had a mixed outcome; 1st- and 2nd-class women almost all survived.
4. Further splits on **Fare** inside the 3rd-class-female branch.

> *Professor's note:* *"I don't see any red flags. There's nothing in this graph that is a surprise given the data exploration I already did. That is exactly what I want."*

## Predicting and evaluating

```r
pr <- predict(rpart_model,
              newdata = titanic_train[test, c(input, target)],
              type = "class")

tab <- table(pr, titanic_train[test, ]$Survived,
             dnn = c("Predicted", "Actual"))
print(tab)
#            Actual
# Predicted   0   1
#         0  50  19
#         1   4  17

accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab)) / sum(tab)
sprintf("Accuracy = %.1f%%", accuracy)
# "Accuracy = 74.4%"
```

---

# The Confusion Matrix

For binary classification, the four possible outcomes are:

|  | **Actual = 0** (did NOT survive) | **Actual = 1** (DID survive) |
|---|---|---|
| **Predicted = 0** | True Negative (**TN**) | False Negative (**FN**) |
| **Predicted = 1** | False Positive (**FP**) | True Positive (**TP**) |

For the Titanic model above:

| Cell | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| TN | 50 | Correctly said "did not survive" |
| FN | 19 | Said "did not survive" but they actually did |
| FP | 4 | Said "survived" but they actually didn't |
| TP | 17 | Correctly said "survived" |

## The accuracy formula

$$\text{Accuracy} \;=\; \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} \;=\; \frac{17 + 50}{17 + 50 + 4 + 19} \;=\; \frac{67}{90} \;\approx\; 74\%$$

## Computing TN/FP/FN/TP in R

```r
testData <- titanic_train[test, ]
testData$pr <- pr

TN <- sum(testData$Survived == 0 & testData$pr == 0)
FP <- sum(testData$Survived == 0 & testData$pr == 1)
FN <- sum(testData$Survived == 1 & testData$pr == 0)
TP <- sum(testData$Survived == 1 & testData$pr == 1)
```

## Class-wise performance

Look at the confusion matrix by row and column:

| | Did NOT survive (Actual = 0) | DID survive (Actual = 1) |
|---|---|---|
| Total in test set | 54 | 36 |
| Correctly classified | 50 (93%) | 17 (47%) |

**The model is much better at spotting non-survivors than survivors.** Overall accuracy of 74% hides this imbalance.

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Exam-style thought question — carry this into Week 4 {#sec-thought-question}

> *Professor's question (paraphrased):* *"Suppose someone builds a model to predict whether a patient has cancer, and reports 90% overall accuracy. Is this a good model? Would you put it in production?"*

**Hint — think about the base rate.** If only 1 in 100 people in the screening population actually has cancer, a model that **always says "no cancer"** will be 99% accurate and **miss every single sick person**. Accuracy alone is dangerous when classes are imbalanced.

Week 4 introduces **precision, recall, and F1** — the metrics that expose this problem.
:::

---

# Handling Missing Values

`Age` has 177 missing values (~20% of the column). Three reasonable options:

| Option | When to use it | Downside |
|---|---|---|
| **Drop the column** | The column is not important and has lots of missing values | Lose a potentially useful predictor |
| **Drop the rows with missing values** | Very few rows affected | Loses data; fails for new rows with missing values at prediction time |
| **Impute (fill in) the missing values** | Default choice for important columns | Imputed values carry no real information, just keep the row usable |

For Titanic's `Age`, imputation is correct.

## Mean imputation — done correctly

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Exam-critical detail

The mean **must be calculated on the training set only**, then applied to both train and test sets.

Calculating the mean on the full dataset (train + test combined) is a form of **data leakage** — information from the test set has bled into the training process.
:::

```r
# Correct: mean is computed from TRAIN only (na.rm = TRUE ignores NAs)
meanAge <- mean(titanic_train[train, ]$Age, na.rm = TRUE)
meanAge
# 29.725 (approximately)

# Apply to the WHOLE dataset — so both train and test rows have Age filled in
titanic_train[is.na(titanic_train$Age), ]$Age <- meanAge
```

After imputation, the Titanic decision tree's accuracy improves from **74.4% to 75.5%** — a small but real gain, and far more important for algorithms like logistic regression that refuse to run with NAs.

## Mode imputation for categorical columns

For the 2 blank `Embarked` values, the same principle applies — use the **mode** (most common category) of the training set:

```r
titanic_train[titanic_train$Embarked == "", ]$Embarked <- 'S'
```

---

# Scenario → Concept Reference Table

Exam-style situations and what to do about them:

| Scenario | Correct action | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| A column has a unique value for every row (e.g. `PassengerId`, `Name`, `Ticket`) | Drop it | No generalisable pattern — tree will memorise |
| A column has 5–10% missing values and is important | Impute (mean for numeric, mode for categorical) on **train only** | Preserves rows; avoids data leakage |
| A column has ~70% missing values (e.g. `Cabin`) | Drop it | Too little real information left |
| Categorical variable encoded as numbers (`Pclass` = 1, 2, 3) | Convert with `as.factor()` | Stop the algorithm doing arithmetic on categories |
| Numeric variable with a threshold effect (e.g. age and children) | Create a derived binary variable | Makes the threshold visible to the algorithm |
| Two predictors are highly correlated (e.g. `Pclass` and `Fare`) | Consider dropping one | Redundant information; algorithm will pick one and ignore the other |
| `Embarked` shows a strong signal but seems implausible | Cross-check against other predictors | Usually it is a proxy for the real cause |
| Test accuracy ≫ training accuracy on a tree | Suspicious — could indicate a buggy split | Rare; investigate before trusting |
| Training accuracy ≫ test accuracy | **Overfitting** — prune the tree or use a simpler model | Classic symptom |
| New categorical values appear at prediction time that weren't in training | Pipeline error — usually means the variable shouldn't have been used | Decision tree cannot handle unseen categories |
| Predictor includes a field only known after the target event (e.g. "card cancelled" when predicting fraud) | Remove it immediately | **Data leakage** — model will be useless in production |

---

# Summary Points — Week 3 Takeaways

- ✅ **Always profile a dataset before modelling**: count unique values and blanks in every column.
- ✅ **Plot counts AND proportions** when comparing a predictor to the target.
- ✅ **Counts show effect size; proportions show signal strength.** You need both.
- ✅ **Create derived variables** (`is_child`, `family`) when raw variables have threshold effects.
- ✅ **Cross-check predictors against each other**, not just against the target. Watch for proxy variables.
- ✅ **Convert ordinal/categorical numeric codes to factors** with `as.factor()`.
- ✅ **Always set a random seed** for reproducible data science.
- ✅ **Split into train and test before anything else.** No row in both. No exceptions.
- ✅ **Fit the model on train only. Evaluate on test only.**
- ✅ **Compute imputation values from the train set only**, then apply to both sets.
- ✅ **Watch for data leakage** — any predictor that would not be available at prediction time must be removed.
- ✅ **Drop ID-like columns** (`PassengerId`, `Ticket`, `Name`, `Cabin`) before fitting a decision tree.
- ✅ **Decision trees are greedy** — they are great for interpretation and poor at capturing variable interactions.
- ✅ **Overall accuracy can mislead.** Always look at the full confusion matrix, especially with imbalanced classes.

Before moving on to logistic regression and ML metrics, make sure you can:

- [ ] Explain the difference between signal strength and effect size.
- [ ] Create a derived variable like `is_child` and justify why it helps.
- [ ] Convert a numeric categorical column to a factor and explain why.
- [ ] Perform a 90/10 train/test split reproducibly using `set.seed` and `sample`.
- [ ] State the three non-negotiable rules of a train/test split.
- [ ] Explain overfitting, data drift, and model drift — and give an example of each.
- [ ] Explain what data leakage is and give an example from outside the lecture.
- [ ] Fit a decision tree with `rpart()` and plot it with `rpart.plot()`.

- [ ] Compute accuracy from a confusion matrix by hand.
- [ ] Explain why 90% accuracy on a cancer-screening model can be **bad**.
